disease.name: Gastric Cancer
disease.note: Often asymptomatic until advanced. Epigastric pain, weight loss, and anorexia are late presentations. In East Africa, late presentation is common due to limited endoscopy access and use of traditional remedies. H. pylori infection remains the dominant cause. High index of suspicion needed in patients over 40 with persistent dyspepsia.
presentingComplaints: Persistent upper abdominal pain not going away
Feeling full after eating only a little
pathophysiologyDetail: Correa cascade: chronic H. pylori gastritis → atrophic gastritis → intestinal metaplasia → low-grade dysplasia → high-grade dysplasia → intestinal-type adenocarcinoma over decades
Lauren classification: intestinal-type (gland-forming, better prognosis, H. pylori-associated) vs diffuse-type (signet-ring cells, infiltrative, worse prognosis, CDH1-associated)
TCGA molecular classification (2014): EBV-positive (9%), microsatellite instability/MSI-high (22%), genomically stable/diffuse (20%), chromosomal instability/CIN/intestinal (50%)
CDH1 germline mutation encoding E-cadherin: loss of cell adhesion → diffuse-type; lifetime gastric cancer risk ~70% in hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (HDGC) syndrome
H. pylori CagA protein: injected via type IV secretion system → SHP-2 phosphatase activation → disruption of cell-cell junctions, proliferation, and chronic inflammation driving carcinogenesis
IL-1β and TNF-α pro-inflammatory cytokine polymorphisms: increase gastric cancer risk 2-3 fold in H. pylori-infected individuals by amplifying inflammatory response
EBV-associated subtype: LMP2A activates PI3K/AKT pathway → CpG island methylator phenotype (CIMP) → epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressor genes
Microsatellite instability: defective DNA mismatch repair (MLH1 silencing or MSH2/MSH6 mutations) → accumulation of frameshift mutations in TGFβRII, BAX, IGFIIR
p53 tumor suppressor loss: TP53 mutation or 17p LOH in CIN subtype → genomic instability, progression from dysplasia to invasive carcinoma
Wnt/β-catenin pathway: aberrant nuclear β-catenin accumulation → transcription of c-myc, cyclin D1, and MMP-7 driving proliferation and invasion in intestinal-type cancers
E-cadherin loss in diffuse-type: CDH1 mutation or promoter hypermethylation → discohesive signet-ring cells infiltrating individually producing linitis plastica (leather-bottle stomach)
VEGF-driven angiogenesis: gastric cancers overexpress VEGF-A, VEGF-C, VEGF-D → tumor neovascularization and lymphangiogenesis enabling hematogenous/lymphatic metastasis
Peritoneal dissemination: free cancer cells from serosa-invading tumors seed peritoneal cavity → Krukenberg tumors (ovarian) and Blumer's shelf (rectal shelf palpable on DRE)
Epigenetic field cancerization: H. pylori-induced chronic inflammation → widespread CpG hypermethylation (p16, MLH1, CDH1) across gastric mucosa → multifocal cancer risk
Host immune microenvironment: CD8+ T-cell infiltration and PD-L1 expression; MSI-high tumors have high neoantigen burden and respond to checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab)
Dietary carcinogenesis: high-salt diet disrupts gastric mucosal barrier synergistically with H. pylori; N-nitroso compounds from smoked/pickled foods act as direct mutagens
Metabolic syndrome and obesity: linked to GEJ/cardia adenocarcinomas via chronic reflux, elevated leptin, and IGF-1 signaling promoting epithelial proliferation
East African context: 60-70% present at stage III-IV, H. pylori seroprevalence >80% in rural Kenya, limited endoscopy access, traditional medicine delays, CDH1 founder effect in some ethnic groups
Post-gastrectomy remnant carcinoma: Billroth II reconstruction >20 years prior, bile reflux, hypochlorhydria, bacterial overgrowth with N-nitroso production
Pernicious anemia: autoimmune metaplastic atrophic gastritis → 2.5-3x elevated risk via the Correa cascade in the setting of vitamin B12 deficiency and hypergastrinemia

landmarkTrials.trial: MAGIC Trial (2006, New England Journal of Medicine)
landmarkTrials.population: 503 patients with resectable gastric or GEJ adenocarcinoma
landmarkTrials.intervention: Perioperative chemotherapy (epirubicin, cisplatin, fluorouracil — ECF) + surgery vs surgery alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: 5-year OS 36% vs 23% (HR 0.75, p=0.009); PFS HR 0.66. Established perioperative chemotherapy as standard for Western populations.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: ECF regimen feasible in Kenyan setting; epirubicin and cisplatin available; supports perioperative approach for resectable disease

landmarkTrials.population: 716 patients with resectable gastric or GEJ adenocarcinoma
landmarkTrials.intervention: FLOT (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, docetaxel) perioperative vs ECF/ECX perioperative
landmarkTrials.outcome: Median OS 50 months vs 35 months (HR 0.77, p=0.012); 3-year OS 57% vs 48%. FLOT is now preferred perioperative regimen.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Docetaxel available at Kenyan tertiary centers; FLOT requires G-CSF support and is more toxic; may not be suitable for all patients

landmarkTrials.population: 1035 patients with stage II-IIIB gastric cancer after D2 gastrectomy (Asian population)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adjuvant capecitabine + oxaliplatin (XELOX) for 6 months vs observation after D2 gastrectomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: 3-year DFS 74% vs 59% (HR 0.56, p<0.0001); 5-year OS 78% vs 69% (HR 0.66, p=0.0015). Established adjuvant XELOX after D2 gastrectomy.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Capecitabine (oral) is logistically easier than IV regimens — suitable for Kenyan setting where infusion access is limited

landmarkTrials.trial: ARTIST Trial (2012, Journal of Clinical Oncology)
landmarkTrials.population: 458 patients with gastric cancer after D2 gastrectomy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Adjuvant chemoradiation (XP + 45 Gy) vs adjuvant chemotherapy alone (XP)
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in 3-year DFS (78% vs 74%, p=0.09). Subgroup analysis: benefit in node-positive disease. ARTIST 2 ongoing.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Radiation facilities limited in Kenya (only 5 functional linear accelerators nationally); chemotherapy-alone approach more practical

landmarkTrials.population: 175 patients with advanced gastric cancer with a single non-curable factor
landmarkTrials.outcome: Median OS 14.3 months vs 16.6 months (HR 1.09, p=0.70). No survival benefit from gastrectomy in advanced disease with non-curable factors.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Strongly relevant — avoids unnecessary gastrectomy in Kenyan patients who present late with metastatic disease; favor chemotherapy alone

landmarkTrials.population: 594 patients with HER2-positive advanced gastric/GEJ cancer
landmarkTrials.outcome: Median OS 13.8 months vs 11.1 months (HR 0.74, p=0.0046). First targeted therapy approved for gastric cancer. 22% of intestinal-type tumors HER2-positive.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Trastuzumab available in Kenya but costly; HER2 testing not routinely available outside Nairobi; limits applicability in resource-limited settings

landmarkTrials.population: Multiple cohorts of advanced gastric/GEJ cancer — KEYNOTE-059 (259 patients), KEYNOTE-061 (592), KEYNOTE-062 (763)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Pembrolizumab ± chemotherapy vs chemotherapy alone in advanced disease
landmarkTrials.outcome: KEYNOTE-059: ORR 13.3% in previously treated PD-L1+ patients. KEYNOTE-062: Pembrolizumab non-inferior to chemo in PD-L1 CPS ≥1; superior in CPS ≥10 (median OS 17.4 vs 10.8 months). MSI-high subgroup: dramatic benefit.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Pembrolizumab available but very expensive; MSI testing not widely available; PD-L1 CPS testing limited to referral centers

landmarkTrials.population: 1581 patients with previously untreated advanced gastric/GEJ/esophageal adenocarcinoma
landmarkTrials.outcome: Median OS 13.8 vs 11.6 months (HR 0.71, p<0.0001) in PD-L1 CPS ≥5. 12-month OS 55% vs 45%. New standard for first-line advanced disease.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Nivolumab increasingly available in Kenya through access programs; PD-L1 testing infrastructure developing

evidenceTable.recommendation: Perioperative FLOT = standard of care for resectable disease in fit patients
Alternative if FLOT contraindicated; well-established regimen
Adjuvant XELOX standard for Asian patients after D2; reasonable for Kenyan patients with D2 resection

evidenceTable.therapy: Trastuzumab + chemo for HER2+ advanced disease (ToGA)
evidenceTable.population: HER2+ (IHC 3+ or FISH+) advanced gastric/GEJ cancer
evidenceTable.recommendation: First-line for HER2+ advanced disease if HER2 testing available
First-line for PD-L1 CPS ≥5 advanced disease if immunotherapy available
Tissue-agnostic approval for MSI-high tumors; dramatic benefit

evidenceTable.population: T1a differentiated adenocarcinoma ≤2 cm, no ulceration
evidenceTable.recommendation: Standard for early gastric cancer meeting Japanese criteria; ESD preferred over EMR

evidenceTable.therapy: D2 vs D1 lymphadenectomy (Dutch trial, 15-year follow-up)
evidenceTable.effect: D2: 35% vs D1: 21% (p=0.004) — with spleen-pancreas preservation
evidenceTable.recommendation: D2 lymphadenectomy with spleen-pancreas preservation = surgical standard

evidenceTable.therapy: Gastrectomy + chemo vs chemo alone in advanced disease (REGATTA)
evidenceTable.population: Advanced gastric cancer with single non-curable factor
evidenceTable.recommendation: No role for gastrectomy in advanced disease; chemotherapy alone

evidenceTable.population: Previously treated advanced gastric cancer (≥2 prior lines)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Third-line option in fit patients; available in Kenya through oncology programs

evidenceTable.therapy: Palliative chemotherapy vs BSC in advanced disease (Cochrane review)
evidenceTable.effect: Chemotherapy prolongs survival by ~6 months vs BSC (HR ~0.37)
evidenceTable.recommendation: Palliative chemotherapy should be offered to all fit patients with advanced disease

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical suspicion — persistent dyspepsia + weight loss in patient >40 years, epigastric mass, or alarm symptoms (dysphagia, vomiting, anemia, GI bleeding)
Step 2: Upper GI endoscopy with multiple biopsies (minimum 6-8 from ulcer edge and base) — gold standard for diagnosis; biopsy protocol: 2 from ulcer edge, 2 from base, 2 from surrounding mucosa
Step 3: Histopathological evaluation — confirm adenocarcinoma, classify by Lauren type (intestinal vs diffuse), grade differentiation, assess for signet-ring cells
Step 4: Immunohistochemistry/Molecular testing — HER2/neu (IHC ± FISH) in all advanced cases; MSI/MMR status; PD-L1 CPS; EBV-ISH if available; CDH1 testing if family history or diffuse type
Step 5: Staging CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast — assess T stage (wall thickness, invasion), N stage (regional nodes), M stage (liver, lung, peritoneum, distant nodes)
Step 6: Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS) if available — precise T staging (depth of invasion) and N staging (perigastric nodes); guides decision for neoadjuvant therapy vs upfront surgery
Step 7: Staging laparoscopy with peritoneal lavage cytology — mandatory before definitive surgery for T3/T4 tumors; detects occult peritoneal carcinomatosis (upstages 15-25% of patients)
Step 8: PET-CT if available — problem-solving for equivocal metastatic lesions; not routinely recommended (lower sensitivity for diffuse-type and peritoneal disease)
Step 9: Multidisciplinary team discussion — surgical oncologist, medical oncologist, radiologist, pathologist, nutritionist; determine stage-appropriate treatment plan
Step 10: Nutritional and performance status assessment — ECOG PS, albumin, weight loss percentage; guides treatment intensity (perioperative chemo candidacy vs palliative approach)

Kenya adaptation: if endoscopy unavailable, upper GI series with barium may suggest mass/filling defect at district level; refer to provincial/territorial hospital for endoscopy; H. pylori test-and-treat is not sufficient to exclude cancer
managementPearls: All patients with suspected gastric cancer should undergo staging laparoscopy before definitive surgery — 15-25% will have occult peritoneal disease that changes management from curative to palliative
D2 lymphadenectomy must include spleen-pancreas preservation (avoid distal pancreatectomy/splenectomy) to avoid excess morbidity without survival benefit (Dutch trial 15-year data)
FLOT regimen is superior to ECF/ECX but requires G-CSF support and close monitoring for febrile neutropenia; in resource-limited settings, ECF remains an acceptable alternative

Proximal tumors and GEJ Siewert type II-III require total gastrectomy with distal esophagectomy; distal tumors can be managed with subtotal gastrectomy — no survival advantage with total gastrectomy for distal tumors
MSI-high gastric cancers have better prognosis stage-for-stage but do not benefit from perioperative chemotherapy (GERCOR/MAGIC subset analysis); immunotherapy is the preferred systemic approach

HER2 testing should be performed on all advanced gastric cancers; trastuzumab + chemotherapy is the standard first-line for HER2+ disease; repeat biopsy of metastatic site if primary tissue unavailable

Post-gastrectomy nutrition is lifelong: vitamin B12 (IM every 3 months lifelong), iron, calcium, vitamin D, and zinc supplementation; dumping syndrome managed with dietary modifications (small meals, avoid simple sugars)

In East Africa, where patients present late with malignant ascites and Krukenberg tumors, upfront chemotherapy (FLOT or XELOX) rather than surgery is the appropriate approach — REGATTA trial supports this
Endoscopic resection (EMR/ESD) is curative for T1a differentiated adenocarcinoma ≤2 cm without ulceration; careful histologic assessment of margins and vertical invasion depth is critical
Adjuvant chemoradiation (ARTIST trial) showed no significant benefit over chemotherapy alone after D2 gastrectomy; radiation can be reserved for R1/R2 resection margins or node-positive disease
In elderly or frail patients (ECOG ≥2), best supportive care with palliative intent is appropriate; single-agent chemotherapy (capecitabine or S-1) may offer palliation with lower toxicity
Feeding jejunostomy at time of gastrectomy is strongly recommended — allows early enteral nutrition, reduces postop weight loss, and provides route for medications
Perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT) should be completed before and after surgery regardless of pathologic response; even complete pathologic responders (pCR) benefit from completing all 6 cycles
Pembrolizumab is approved for MSI-high gastric cancers regardless of line of therapy; MSI testing should be prioritized over PD-L1 CPS testing when resources limit molecular workup
operativeNuances: Laparoscopic gastrectomy (totally laparoscopic or laparoscopic-assisted) reduces blood loss, pain, and hospital stay compared to open approach; equivalent oncologic outcomes in experienced hands (KLASS-01, CLASS-01 trials)

D2 lymphadenectomy should en bloc remove stations 1-12 with preservation of spleen and pancreas unless directly invaded; station 10 (splenic hilar nodes) only required for proximal tumors invading greater curvature

Proximal margin of at least 5 cm is required for intestinal-type tumors; 8 cm for diffuse-type tumors due to submucosal spread; intraoperative frozen section of proximal margin should be obtained when available
Roux-en-Y reconstruction after total gastrectomy: esophagojejunostomy with 40-60 cm Roux limb reduces bile reflux; hand-sewn vs stapled depends on surgeon preference and access — both acceptable

Billroth II (Polya) reconstruction after subtotal gastrectomy is simpler but associated with bile reflux gastritis; Roux-en-Y conversion may be needed for severe reflux; Braun enteroenterostomy reduces bile reflux

Omental bursa (lesser sac) must be fully opened during dissection to access posterior gastric wall, left gastric artery (station 7), and celiac axis (station 9); failure to do so compromises D2 dissection

In GEJ Siewert type II tumors, the optimal approach is controversial: transhiatal esophagectomy vs transthoracic esophagectomy vs extended total gastrectomy; Ivor-Lewis esophagectomy preferred for >2 cm esophageal involvement
Kocher maneuver is essential for adequate duodenal mobilization and safe duodenal stump closure after subtotal gastrectomy; insecure duodenal stump closure leads to the dreaded duodenal stump blowout

Splenic hilar dissection (station 10) carries risk of splenic capsule tear and subsequent splenectomy; if spleen is injured, attempt splenorrhaphy first; splenectomy increases postop infectious complications

In East Africa, where staplers may be unavailable or cost-prohibitive, hand-sewn anastomosis with absorbable suture (3-0 PDS or Maxon) is safe and effective; single-layer interrupted technique is standard

Feeding jejunostomy should be placed 30-40 cm distal to the ligament of Treitz using Witzel tunnel technique; secure to anterior abdominal wall to prevent torsion; mark with suture for radiologic identification

prognosis.earlyGastricCancer_t1n0: 5-year OS >95% with appropriate treatment (EMR/ESD for T1a, gastrectomy for T1b) — essentially curable if detected early
prognosis.locallyAdvanced_t2_t4_n0_n3: 5-year OS 20-60% depending on T/N stage: T2N0 ~60%, T3N1 ~35%, T4aN2 ~25%, T4bN3 ~10%; perioperative chemotherapy improves OS by ~15% absolute
prognosis.metastatic_m1: Median OS 8-12 months with palliative chemotherapy; 14-16 months with HER2-targeted therapy if HER2+; 12-18 months with immunotherapy if PD-L1 CPS ≥5 or MSI-high
prognosticFactors: TNM stage (AJCC 8th edition) — single most important prognostic factor; pathologic stage after neoadjuvant therapy (ypStage) also highly prognostic
Lauren classification — diffuse-type has 15-20% worse 5-year OS compared to intestinal-type stage-for-stage
MSI status — MSI-high status confers better prognosis (HR 0.6-0.7) independent of stage; associated with distal location, intestinal type, and older age
HER2 status — HER2+ associated with more aggressive disease but targeted therapy (trastuzumab) improves outcomes
Resection margin status — R0 resection (microscopically negative) essential for cure; R1/R2 margins confer survival similar to unresectable disease
Lymph node ratio — ratio of positive to examined nodes predicts survival better than absolute N stage in some studies; >20% positive nodes = poor prognosis
Peritoneal cytology — positive peritoneal cytology (CY+) even without visible peritoneal disease is equivalent to M1 disease; median OS 12-18 months
Histologic grade — poorly differentiated tumors have worse prognosis; signet-ring cell histology is an independent negative predictor
Nutritional status — preoperative weight loss >10% and albumin <3.0 g/dL predict poor outcomes and chemotherapy intolerance
Tumor regression grade (TRG) after neoadjuvant therapy — Mandard/Bechler score; TRG 1-2 (complete/near-complete regression) associated with excellent prognosis
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: 60-70% of Kenyan patients present at stage III-IV; median OS in palliative setting 6-10 months; late presentation limited by endoscopy access and traditional medicine use; HIV co-infection not clearly associated with worse gastric cancer outcomes

history_questions.question: How long have you had upper abdominal pain?
Has the pain changed or gotten worse over time?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past 3-6 months?
Do you feel full after eating only a small amount?
Have you lost your appetite for food?
Do you vomit after meals? What does the vomit look like?
Have you noticed your stools are black or you have vomited blood?
Do you have difficulty swallowing food?
Has anyone in your family had stomach cancer?
Do you smoke cigarettes or use tobacco?
Do you drink alcohol — chang'aa, busaa, or bottled beer?
Do you eat a lot of roasted meat, fried foods, or foods with salt?
Do you eat vegetables and fruits regularly?
Have you ever been treated for H. pylori or stomach ulcers?
Have you had an endoscopy (camera down the throat) before?
Do you use traditional herbal medicines for your stomach?
Do you feel tired or weak?
Have you noticed any lumps in your neck or under your arm?
Do you have any abdominal swelling?
Have you ever had surgery on your stomach before?
Have you ever been tested for H. pylori infection?
Do you have a history of long-standing heartburn or reflux?
Do you feel your food getting stuck when swallowing?
Have you noticed any swelling in your abdomen?
Have you been told you are anemic or have low blood?
Do you have any family members with colon or stomach cancer?
Have you ever had a stomach ulcer diagnosed?
Do you work on a farm or in an area with limited access to fresh vegetables?
What type of cooking oil do you use? Do you eat a lot of fried foods?
Do you have persistent hiccups? (may indicate GEJ involvement)
Have you noticed any change in your bowel habits?
Do you drink untreated water from rivers or wells?
Have you been treated for tuberculosis in the past? (weight loss differential)
Do you have abdominal pain that wakes you up at night?
Is there a family history of breast or ovarian cancer? (CDH1/HDGC syndrome)
Have you had a recent HIV test? (gastric lymphoma differential)
Are you pregnant or could you be pregnant? (if female, Krukenberg tumor consideration)
Do you take traditional herbal remedies for your stomach pain?
Have you ever had a blood transfusion before?

examination_findings.finding: Epigastric mass — firm, irregular, possibly tender
Pallor from iron deficiency anemia
Cachexia — muscle wasting, loss of subcutaneous fat
Left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow's node)
Hepatomegaly from metastases
Ascites — shifting dullness, fluid thrill
Umbilical nodule (Sister Mary Joseph nodule)
Rectal Blumer's shelf — rectal mass on DRE from peritoneal seeding
Left axillary lymph node (Irish node)
Krukenberg tumor — palpable adnexal mass on pelvic exam in women
Abdominal distension from ascites or mass
Succussion splash if gastric outlet obstruction
Dermatomyositis rash (paraneoplastic)
Acanthosis nigricans (paraneoplastic)
Palpable left supraclavicular node (Virchow's/Troisier sign) — firm, fixed, non-tender
Periumbilical nodule (Sister Mary Joseph) — firm, irregular, suggests peritoneal spread
Shifting dullness and fluid thrill from malignant ascites
Blumer's shelf on digital rectal exam — hard, fixed shelf in rectovesical/rectouterine pouch
Left axillary node (Irish node) — less common but pathognomonic when present
Palpable ovarian mass on bimanual pelvic exam (Krukenberg tumor) — bilateral in 40%
Trousseau sign — migratory superficial thrombophlebitis (paraneoplastic hypercoagulability)
Cachexia: temporal wasting, loss of thenar/hypothenar eminences, interosseous muscle wasting
Palpable left lobe liver (hepatomegaly) — firm, nodular suggests metastases
Succession splash on auscultation after shaking patient — indicates gastric outlet obstruction
Pallor of conjunctiva, palms, and nail beds from iron deficiency anemia
Jaundice (rare in gastric cancer unless extensive liver metastases or porta hepatis nodes)
Subcutaneous nodules — cutaneous metastases (rare, very advanced)
Peripheral edema from hypoalbuminemia or IVC compression by nodes
Oral thrush — immunosuppression from malnutrition or chemotherapy

bedside_scores.name: ECOG Performance Status
bedside_scores.interpretation: ECOG 0-1: candidate for surgery/chemotherapy; ECOG 2+: consider palliative approach

riskFactors.note: Correa cascade: gastritis → metaplasia → dysplasia → cancer
Bilroth II >20 years ago — remnant carcinoma
Hereditary diffuse gastric cancer — 70% lifetime risk
Linked to GEJ and cardia cancers
Linked to H. pylori transmission, limited access to endoscopy

subtypes.name: Intestinal-Type Adenocarcinoma (Lauren)
subtypes.management: Gastrectomy (subtotal or total) with D2 lymphadenectomy. Perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT regimen: 5-FU, Leucovorin, Oxaliplatin, Docetaxel).

subtypes.name: Diffuse-Type Adenocarcinoma (Lauren)
subtypes.management: Total gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy. Perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT). Prophylactic total gastrectomy for CDH1 carriers. Limited response to chemotherapy.

subtypes.name: Gastroesophageal Junction Cancer (Siewert I-III)
subtypes.management: Siewert I-II: Esophagectomy + proximal gastrectomy. Siewert III: Total gastrectomy + distal esophagectomy. Neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS) or perioperative FLOT.

subtypes.name: MSI-High and EBV-Positive Gastric Cancer (TCGA Subtypes)
subtypes.management: MSI-high: Checkpoint inhibitor therapy (pembrolizumab) ± chemotherapy; may not benefit from perioperative chemotherapy. EBV+: Consider immunotherapy; less benefit from conventional chemotherapy. Both subtypes have better prognosis. MSI testing by IHC (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) or PCR.

mimics.whyMimic: Epigastric pain, dyspepsia, can cause weight loss
Gastric mass, epigastric pain, weight loss
Can present as gastric mass with/without bleeding
Gastric mass, can cause bleeding and obstruction
Gastric mass, epigastric pain, weight loss, H. pylori association
Epigastric pain, weight loss, anorexia, thickened gastric folds on imaging
Right upper quadrant/epigastric pain, fever, weight loss in endemic areas
Epigastric discomfort, early satiety, weight loss, gastric wall thickening
Abdominal swelling, weight loss, anorexia, ascites, epigastric mass-like omental cake
Epigastric mass, early satiety, vomiting from extrinsic compression

bedside.name: Fecal occult blood test
bedside.indication: May be positive from chronic blood loss

laboratory.name: CBC
laboratory.indication: Anemia from chronic blood loss or iron deficiency

laboratory.name: Iron studies
laboratory.indication: Iron deficiency anemia — common presenting sign

laboratory.name: Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9, CA 72-4)
laboratory.indication: Monitoring response to therapy; not diagnostic

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: Nutritional status — low albumin predicts poor outcome

laboratory.name: H. pylori testing (stool Ag or serology)

imaging.name: Upper GI Endoscopy with biopsy
imaging.indication: Gold standard — visualize lesion, biopsy for histology

imaging.name: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Staging, detect metastases, assess tumor extent

imaging.name: Endoscopic Ultrasound (EUS)
imaging.indication: T and N staging — depth of invasion, nodal status

imaging.name: Staging laparoscopy
imaging.indication: Detect peritoneal metastases before definitive surgery

resuscitation: Correct anemia — blood transfusion if Hb <7 or symptomatic
IV fluids if dehydrated from vomiting or poor intake
NG tube for gastric decompression if outlet obstruction
Nutritional support — dietician review, consider NG/TN if severely malnourished
Pain control — avoid NSAIDs, use paracetamol ± morphine
conservative: Proton pump inhibitor for symptom control

surgical.step1: Perioperative chemotherapy: FLOT regimen (3 cycles pre-op, 3 cycles post-op)
surgical.step2: Staging laparoscopy to rule out peritoneal disease
surgical.step3: Subtotal gastrectomy for distal tumors (antrum, body) — 5 cm proximal margin
surgical.step4: Total gastrectomy for proximal, mid-body, or diffuse tumors
surgical.step5: D2 lymphadenectomy: perigastric + celiac, hepatic, splenic, left gastric nodes
surgical.step6: Roux-en-Y reconstruction (total gastrectomy) or Billroth II (subtotal)

nonOperative: Endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) or submucosal dissection (ESD): for T1a differentiated tumors <2 cm
Definitive chemoradiation: for unresectable localized disease
Palliative chemotherapy: for metastatic disease (FLOT, FOLFOX, XP)

general: ICU or HDU monitoring for 24-48 hours
NG tube on free drainage — remove day 3-5
Feeding jejunostomy: start day 1 at 20 mL/hr, advance to goal by day 3-5
Drains: remove when output <50 mL/day serous
Pain control — epidural or multimodal analgesia
diet_advancement: Day 0-2: NBM — enteral feeding via jejunostomy
Day 3-5: Sips of water, clear fluids if NG removed
Day 5-7: Soft diet — pureed, semisolid foods
Day 7-10: Soft solids — well-cooked rice, mashed potatoes, eggs
Lifelong: small frequent meals (6-8/day), avoid large volumes
Avoid: concentrated sugars (dumping syndrome), large meals

pathways.criteria: Early gastric cancer, confined to mucosa/submucosa, no node involvement
pathways.treatment: EMR/ESD for select cases. Otherwise gastrectomy with D1 lymphadenectomy. No neoadjuvant therapy needed.

pathways.criteria: Invasion beyond submucosa, node positive, no metastases
pathways.treatment: Perioperative chemotherapy (FLOT). Total/subtotal gastrectomy with D2 lymphadenectomy.
Palliative chemotherapy (FLOT, FOLFOX, or XP). Palliative surgery for obstruction/bleeding. Best supportive care.

operative_templates.procedure: Radical Subtotal Gastrectomy with D2 Lymphadenectomy
steps: Supine position, midline laparotomy or laparoscopic
Abdominal exploration — assess for peritoneal metastases
Mobilize greater curvature — preserve right gastroepiploic arcade
Divide gastrocolic ligament, enter lesser sac
Divide left gastric artery at origin (lymph node station 7)
Transect stomach with 5 cm proximal margin from tumor

dischargeCriteria: Tolerating soft diet without vomiting or dumping symptoms
No evidence of leak or bleeding
Understanding of dietary restrictions and small frequent meals
B12 injection and iron supplement plan in place (if total gastrectomy)

patient_education.explanation: Stomach cancer is a growth in the lining of your stomach. It is often linked to H. pylori infection, smoking, and diet. Symptoms may be mild at first but can become severe as the tumor grows. Treatment involves surgery to remove part or all of the stomach, often with chemotherapy before and after.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if: you vomit blood or coffee-ground material, you pass black stools, you have severe abdominal pain, you develop a fever with chills, you cannot keep fluids down, or you have worsening abdominal swelling.
patient_education.activity: After surgery, start walking as soon as possible. Avoid heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks. You may tire easily — rest between activities. Most people return to normal activities in 6-12 weeks.
patient_education.diet: Eat small, frequent meals (6-8 per day). Eat slowly and chew food well. Do not drink too much with meals. Avoid sugary foods and drinks (prevent dumping syndrome). Good choices: porridge (uji), rice, mashed potatoes, eggs, fish, chicken, well-cooked vegetables, bananas. Avoid: fried foods, very spicy foods, alcohol, carbonated drinks.
patient_education.medication: You may need vitamin B12 injections every 3 months if your whole stomach was removed. Take iron, calcium, and vitamin D supplements as prescribed. Take pain medication as needed. Chemotherapy will be arranged by the oncology team.
patient_education.followUp: First visit at 2 weeks for wound check and histology results. Then every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 3 years, then annually. CT scans every 6-12 months for surveillance. Blood tests including vitamin levels.

before_discharge: Dietary teaching: small frequent meals, avoid sugars
Vitamin B12 injection scheduled (if total gastrectomy)
Feeding jejunostomy care teaching if applicable
Oncology referral for adjuvant chemotherapy plan

after_discharge.action: Surgical clinic — wound check, histology results discussion
Oncology review — start adjuvant chemotherapy if indicated
CT scan for restaging. Nutritional assessment. Vitamin levels.
Clinical review, CT scan. Monitor for recurrence.
Annual surveillance: CT, endoscopy if indicated, nutritional status

when_to_return: Vomiting or inability to keep fluids down
Vomiting blood or passing black stools
Severe diarrhea or dumping symptoms not controlled with diet
New lumps or masses in the abdomen or neck
